plotting_plan.title: RC Anti-Aliasing Filter Bode Plot
plotting_plan.objective: Demonstrate the frequency response of the RC anti-aliasing filter, showing cutoff frequency and attenuation at specific frequencies (e.g., 60 Hz and 250 Hz). This visually proves the filter's effectiveness in attenuating noise while passing relevant signal frequencies.

plotting_plan.title: Mixed-Signal Neural Clamp Benchmark Plots
plotting_plan.objective: A collection of plots illustrating key performance metrics for the mixed-signal neural clamp, including timeline, membrane voltage/current, amplifier saturation, ADC/digital logic behavior, parameter sweep results, and fault-injection outcomes. This visualizes the system's transient response, limitations, and sensitivity.

plotting_plan.title: CiTT Framework Overview
plotting_plan.objective: A high-level diagram illustrating the components of the CiTT system: Gemini-assisted prompt/image parsing, agentic Simulink/Simscape model generation (SATK/MCP), Simscape/Simulink artifact checks, Socratic teaching loop, model highlight/zoom, natural-language probe maps, and evidence export. This provides a conceptual understanding of the system architecture.

plotting_plan.title: Simscape Model of RC Anti-Aliasing Filter
plotting_plan.objective: A screenshot of the generated Simscape model for the RC anti-aliasing filter. This visually confirms the executable artifact generation capability of the CiTT system.

plotting_plan.title: Simscape Model of Two-Electrode Voltage Clamp
plotting_plan.objective: A screenshot of the generated Simscape model for the two-electrode voltage clamp circuit. This visually confirms the system's ability to represent and generate complex circuit models.

plotting_plan.title: Simscape Model of Mixed-Signal Neural Clamp
plotting_plan.objective: A screenshot of the generated Simscape model for the mixed-signal neural clamp. This visually demonstrates the system's capability to handle mixed-signal and more intricate circuit designs.

introduction_strategy.hook_hypothesis: Biomedical engineering education relies heavily on understanding complex electrical circuits and signal processing concepts, which are foundational for medical device development.
introduction_strategy.problem_gap_hypothesis: Current LLM-based tutoring for engineering subjects often lacks the rigor of executable simulations and inspectable models, leading to potential errors in unit handling, assumption justification, and the omission of critical non-ideal behaviors. This gap hinders deep engineering understanding and reliable design practice.
search_directions: Survey papers on the role of simulation in engineering education
Recent reviews on AI/LLMs in STEM education
Foundational papers on circuit analysis and simulation tools in biomedical engineering
Papers discussing the challenges of teaching signal processing in BME curricula

related_work_strategy.overview: Investigate existing approaches to AI-driven tutoring and engineering problem-solving, categorizing them by their reliance on formal models and executable artifacts.

subsections.sota_investigation_mission: Identify recent (2023-2026) LLM-based educational tools for STEM, focusing on their methodologies for problem-solving and explanation generation. Determine their limitations in handling quantitative engineering problems.
subsections.limitation_hypothesis: These methods rely solely on the LLM's linguistic capabilities, which can lead to factual inaccuracies, unit errors, and a lack of grounded, verifiable engineering artifacts.
limitation_search_queries: Unit error analysis in LLM problem solving
subsections.bridge_to_our_method: CiTT augments LLM communication with executable Simscape models, ensuring engineering rigor and verifiable outputs.

subsections.sota_investigation_mission: Find research papers on symbolic circuit analysis tools and their application in education. Assess their ability to handle complex, non-linear, or mixed-signal circuits and provide intuitive learning experiences.
subsections.limitation_hypothesis: Symbolic tools often struggle with complex, real-world circuit behaviors (e.g., non-idealities, dynamic systems) and lack the interactive, visual feedback mechanisms that facilitate learning for students.
limitation_search_queries: Symbolic circuit analysis limitations complex circuits
Teaching non-linear circuits with symbolic methods
subsections.bridge_to_our_method: CiTT integrates symbolic reasoning with dynamic simulation and visual feedback through Simulink/Simscape.

subsections.sota_investigation_mission: Examine existing integrated environments (e.g., MATLAB/Simulink, COMSOL) for circuit design and simulation. Identify how they are used in educational contexts and their limitations in providing agentic tutoring capabilities.
subsections.limitation_hypothesis: While powerful for simulation, these platforms often require significant user expertise to set up and interpret, and lack the natural language interaction and guided learning features of an agentic tutor.
limitation_search_queries: Bridging user interaction and simulation in engineering education
subsections.bridge_to_our_method: CiTT leverages MATLAB/Simulink/Simscape but adds an agentic layer for natural language interaction and guided exploration.

content_bullets: Highlight the challenge of teaching complex biomedical circuits to students who increasingly rely on LLMs.
Introduce CiTT, a MATLAB-native agentic tutor grounding LLM communication in executable Simscape models.
Describe the core components: Gemini-assisted parsing, agentic model generation via SATK/MCP, Socratic dialogue, and evidence export.
Summarize key results across three benchmarks (RC anti-aliasing, TEVC, mixed-signal neural clamp), emphasizing inspectable artifacts, model-grounded teaching, and evidence generation.
State the contribution of a novel model-grounded authority structure for AI tutoring in engineering.
Discuss the importance of circuit analysis and signal processing in biomedical engineering education and medical device development.
Identify the limitations of traditional LLMs as standalone tutors for rigorous engineering subjects, citing issues like unit errors, unsupported assumptions, and lack of executable validation.
State the need for an AI tutor that combines natural language interaction with verifiable engineering artifacts.
Introduce CiTT (Circuit and Instrumentation Tutor) as a novel agentic tutor.
Explain its core principle: grounding LLM communication in executable MATLAB/Simulink/Simscape models.
Briefly outline the system's workflow and its key benefits: enhancing learning through interactive models and verifiable evidence.

List the claimed contributions: (1) MATLAB-native agentic tutor workflow for biomedical circuits, (2) Model-grounded authority structure separating LLM communication from executable evidence, (3) Live evidence across three benchmarks (RC anti-aliasing, TEVC, mixed-signal neural clamp), and (4) A submission-oriented evidence export path.
Emphasize that this work is positioned as an educational software draft for course/design-competition review.
Discuss existing LLM applications in education, highlighting their strengths in natural language processing and explanation generation.

Detail their limitations when applied to quantitative engineering domains, such as lack of simulation capabilities, susceptibility to factual errors, and inability to produce verifiable artifacts. Mention the 'LLM-only baseline' performance from experimental logs.
citation_hints: research paper or technical report introducing 'Gemini'

Discuss established platforms like MATLAB/Simulink/Simscape for circuit simulation and modeling.

Acknowledge their power in executing simulations but highlight the steep learning curve and the absence of agentic tutoring or natural language interaction for guided learning. Reference the use of MATLAB and Simulink in the proposed system.
